manifest.description: Blueprint-compiled draft dispatch pack for math.meas.sigma-algebra (Phase 1 front-end; DRAFT only)

assets.title: Learning Objectives
assets.content:
- LO1: Define a **σ-algebra** $\mathcal{F}$ on a set $X$ as a collection of subsets of $X$ satisfying three closure properties: (i) $X\in\mathcal{F}$, (ii) closed under complement ($A\in\mathcal{F}\Rightarrow A^c\in\mathcal{F}$), (iii) closed under **countable** union ($A_1,A_2,\ldots\in\mathcal{F}\Rightarrow \bigcup_i A_i\in\mathcal{F}$) — and derive that closure under countable intersection follows from (ii) and (iii) via De Morgan's laws.
- LO2: Explain why the σ-algebra requirement (as opposed to "every subset is measurable") becomes necessary precisely when $X$ is **uncountable** — connecting back to the finite/countable sample-space case where every subset trivially qualifies.
- LO3: Recognize the **Borel σ-algebra** $\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$ as the σ-algebra generated by the open sets of $\mathbb{R}$, and identify $(X,\mathcal{F})$ as a **measurable space** — the structure on which a measure (or probability) can later be defined.

assets.title: PARTIAL-AXIOM-CHECK
assets.content: | MC-1 | PARTIAL-AXIOM-CHECK | Verifying only that $X$ (and perhaps $\emptyset$) belongs to a candidate collection, without checking complement/union closure for every member, and concluding it's a σ-algebra prematurely | Foundational |

assets.title: SIGMA-ALGEBRA-SEEN-AS-UNNECESSARY-FORMALISM
assets.content: | MC-2 | SIGMA-ALGEBRA-SEEN-AS-UNNECESSARY-FORMALISM | Believing the σ-algebra restriction is arbitrary extra bookkeeping rather than a genuine mathematical necessity that specifically arises for uncountable sets | Foundational |

assets.title: COUNTABLE-UNION-MISTAKEN-FOR-ARBITRARY-UNION
assets.content: | MC-3 | COUNTABLE-UNION-MISTAKEN-FOR-ARBITRARY-UNION | Believing σ-algebras must be closed under *arbitrary* (possibly uncountable) unions, not just countable ones — leading to incorrect claims about what must be included | Moderate |

assets.title: The Three Closure Axioms (Primitive P11: Representation Shift)
Finite/Countable vs. Uncountable X (Primitive P06: Contrast Pair)
Mastery Gate (Primitive P91)
assets.content:
### Teaching Action A03 — Mastery Gate (Primitive P91)

P91 = P77 → P55 → P76 → P55 → P75 → P55 → P74 → P55 → P78.

- **P77 (4-problem set)**:
  1. Verify whether $\mathcal{F}=\{\emptyset, \{a\}, \{b,c,d\}, \{a,b,c,d\}\}$ is a valid σ-algebra on $X=\{a,b,c,d\}$.
  2. Given axioms 1 (contains $X$) and 2 (closed under complement) plus 3 (closed under countable union), derive that $\emptyset\in\mathcal{F}$ always (hint: apply axiom 2 to $X$ itself).
  3. Using De Morgan's law, show explicitly why closure under complement + countable union together imply closure under countable intersection.
  4. Explain in your own words why a finite sample space never needs anything beyond its full power set, but an uncountable one does.
- **P76 (Transfer Probe, mode = cross-link probe against `math.prob.event`)**: "Recall from your `math.prob.event` lesson the poker-chip die-roll example: $\Omega=\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$, with event $A=\{2,4,6\}$ (even numbers), and that lesson explicitly noted that for this finite $\Omega$, every subset qualifies as an event — deferring the σ-algebra restriction as 'advanced, for continuous sample spaces.' Now that you've learned σ-algebras properly: (a) verify that the full power set of $\Omega=\{1,\ldots,6\}$ genuinely does satisfy all three σ-algebra axioms (you don't need to list all 64 subsets — argue it structurally). (b) Now suppose instead the sample space were $\Omega'=[0,1]$ (a spinner landing anywhere on a continuous dial from 0 to 1) — explain concretely why 'every subset is an event,' which worked fine for the die, is no longer a mathematically safe assumption, and what structure (named in this lesson) replaces it."
- **P55 (Score)** after each stage.
- **P75 (Mastery Assessment against MAMR = 5/5)**.
- **P74 (Routing Decision)**: MAMR met → P78 (Completion). MAMR not met → route to the specific missed primitive's repair action before re-attempting P77.

assets.title: Prerequisite Check
assets.content: Assumes mastery of `math.found.set-theory` (subsets, complements, unions, intersections, countability). This concept also directly builds on and generalizes the sibling concept `math.prob.event`, which already introduced the *motivating* idea (events as subsets to which probability is assigned) at a finite/countable level and explicitly flagged σ-algebras as the necessary generalization for uncountable sample spaces — see Component 7.

assets.title: Cross-Blueprint Dependencies
assets.content:
- **Requires**: `math.found.set-theory` (subsets, complements, unions/intersections, and the notion of countability, all directly used in the axioms).
- **Unlocks**: `math.meas.measure` (a measure is a function defined precisely on the sets of a σ-algebra, extending this concept's structural foundation with an actual size/probability assignment).
- **Cross-link**: KG lists `math.prob.event` as a cross-link. Verified via directory listing that `docs/curriculum/blueprints/math.prob.event.md` **is already authored** — and, read directly (via grep of its Components), that blueprint's own P76_mode was deliberately set to "independence" specifically because at the time of its authoring, σ-algebras were not yet available, with an explicit deferred note: "for uncountable Ω, events must form a σ-algebra... non-Tier-1; not assessed in this blueprint." This blueprint is the natural closing of that loop: the P76 transfer probe above directly reuses `math.prob.event`'s own die-roll/poker-chip example and its own deferred question, giving the learner the promised follow-up.
